session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_title: In Vivo CAR T Cell Platforms and Paradigms

biomarkers.name: MRD
biomarkers.finding: Patients treated with in vivo BCMA CAR-T achieved MRD negative status at day 60.

clinical_data.value: stringent complete response or very good partial response
5/5 patients had grade 3 adverse events (3 grade 3 CRS, 1 grade 2 CRS, 1 grade 1 ICANS)

MOA_summary: The speaker proposes shifting from ex vivo CAR-T manufacturing to in vivo generation using targeted viral-like particles (VLPs) or lipid nanoparticles (LNPs). By blinding the VSV glycoprotein and adding targeting ligands (e.g., anti-CD7 or anti-TCR), the system selectively transduces T cells in vivo, bypassing the need for lymphodepleting chemotherapy and complex GMP facilities.
key_findings: In vivo BCMA CAR-T generation via TCR-targeted VLPs led to stringent complete or very good partial responses in 4 out of 5 multiple myeloma patients.
In vivo BCMA CAR-T therapy showed a bimodal toxicity profile: an initial infusion reaction followed by traditional cytokine release syndrome (CRS).
mRNA-LNP delivery of anti-CD19 CARs achieved transient B-cell aplasia and reduction in SLEDAI scores in refractory SLE patients.
Preclinical NHP data showed persistent B-cell aplasia following CD7-targeted VLP delivery of CAR20, though anti-CAR antibodies developed in 7-14 days.
Calculations suggest that only 0.05% to 2% transduction efficiency of total body T cells is required to reach the therapeutic dose of ~200 million CAR-positive cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Immunogenicity of the CAR construct can lead to the development of anti-CAR antibodies, which may cause B-cells to return (observed in NHP models).
open_questions: Whether tissue-resident immune effector cells in lymphoid organs are better suited for cancer immunotherapy than circulating blood T cells.
The exact cause of early inflammatory toxicity in in vivo VLP delivery (innate immune response vs. CD3 cross-linking).

session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_title: CAR T Cells for Pediatric Brain Tumors

biomarkers.name: anti-CAR antibodies
biomarkers.finding: Anti-CAR antibody levels in plasma were inversely related to CAR T cell persistence and directly impeded CAR killing.

biomarkers.name: cell-free DNA
biomarkers.finding: cfDNA recurrence correlated with the disappearance of circulating CAR T cells.

models.name: Arm A, B, and C trials
models.genotype_or_key_features: Pediatric patients with H3-mutant diffuse midline glioma

clinical_data.value: favorable median overall survival (compared to historical 9-11 months)

MOA_summary: The study evaluates GD2-targeted CAR T cells for H3-mutant diffuse midline gliomas. The research demonstrates that lymphodepletion is critical for CAR T persistence; without it, patients develop robust anti-CAR antibody and T-cell responses (including IgA in the CNS) that reject the therapy. Re-introducing lymphodepletion every four cycles (Arm C) restored durable responses.
key_findings: Intracerebroventricular (ICV) delivery is 10-fold more potent than IV delivery in preclinical models.
Arm A (IV + ICV + lymphodepletion) showed significant tumor shrinkage and improved OS, but high-grade CRS occurred after IV infusion.
Arm B (ICV only, no lymphodepletion) resulted in transient responses (<3 months) due to rapid immune rejection of CAR T cells.
Anti-CAR responses include CD4+ (IL-2, IFN-gamma) and CD8+ (degranulating, 4-1BB+) T cells, as well as IgA antibodies produced by B cells within the CNS.
Arm C (ICV + lymphodepletion every 4 cycles) resulted in durable responses with median OS not reached and a plateau of >50% survival.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Immunogenicity of the mouse-derived scFv and junctional domains (4-1BB/zeta) triggers robust host immune rejection.
IgA antibodies in the CNS suggest that the immune response is initiated within the CNS or peri-CNS tissues.

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent the emergence of anti-CAR antibodies by depleting B cells

open_questions: Whether myeloid cells in the CNS contribute to a second axis of resistance.
Whether stealth platforms (used in allogeneic CARs) could prevent rejection in autologous CARs for solid tumors.

session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_title: Precision Targeted Engineering of CAR T Cells
MOA_summary: The speaker describes a CRISPR HDR knock-in strategy to create 'armored' CAR T cells that secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-12, IL-2) under the control of endogenous tumor-specific promoters (NR4A2, RGS16). This approach ensures that potent cytokines are expressed only at the tumor site, reducing systemic toxicity compared to NFAT-inducible promoters while enhancing T cell polyfunctionality and host anti-tumor immunity.
key_findings: NR4A2 is highly tumor-restricted and safe for driving high-toxicity transgenes like IL-12, whereas RGS16 is more potent but less restricted.
IL-12 driven by NR4A2 showed potent anti-tumor effects without the systemic weight loss seen with RGS16 or PD-1 promoters.
IL-2 driven by the RGS16 promoter showed the strongest anti-tumor effect and was well-tolerated.
A 'next-generation' knock-in strategy using a P2A linker to preserve endogenous RGS16 expression significantly reduced peripheral GFP expression while maintaining high tumor expression.
The one-step CRISPR knock-in of both the armoring gene and the CAR into the TRAC locus increased CAR expression without reducing transgene output.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Disruption of the RGS16 gene through standard CRISPR knock-in led to a loss of gene regulation and increased peripheral expression; this was corrected using a P2A linker strategy.
open_questions: Whether this strategy can be used to deliver chemokines (CXCL9, CXCL10) to increase T cell trafficking to the tumor.
